QF.id: Analysis of Equity and Debt Instruments_24

question.text: For an eligible start-up, when is the perquisite tax on ESOPs deferred to under the special provision?

answer.text: The perquisite tax is permanently exempt for start-up ESOPs
Deferred until 3 years from the date of exercise
Deferred to the earliest of: 5 years from allotment, date of sale of shares, or date of cessation of employment
Deferred until the date of sale of shares only

explanation.text: **The Logic:** For eligible start-ups, the perquisite tax on ESOPs (the difference between FMV on exercise date and exercise price) is deferred. The tax becomes payable on the EARLIEST of three events: (1) expiry of 5 years from the date of allotment, (2) the date the employee sells the shares, or (3) the date the employee ceases employment with the start-up. This deferral addresses the liquidity problem — start-up employees had to pay tax on paper gains before having cash from selling shares. **The Trap:** Option A suggests permanent exemption — the tax is only deferred, not exempted. Option B incorrectly says 3 years (it's 5 years). Option D captures only one trigger event. The three-trigger framework is critical — many start-up employees forget that leaving the company also triggers the deferred tax. **Cross-Exam Context:** Start-up ESOP taxation is a specialized topic in NISM X-B. The broader ESOP two-stage taxation (perquisite + capital gains) is tested across X-A and X-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Advise start-up employees to plan for the deferred tax liability. If they leave the company before selling shares or before 5 years, the perquisite tax becomes immediately due — even without any cash realization. Build this into their financial plan.
